edges.description: UV photoproducts persist because the canonical XP repair network is defective.
POLH deficiency creates an alternative route to XP through failed lesion bypass during replication.
edges.target: Defective error-free translesion synthesis in XP-V

edges.description: Persistent DNA lesions increase the mutational load in sun-exposed tissues.
edges.target: UV-signature mutagenesis and tumor mutation burden

edges.description: Failed repair of UV-induced DNA damage produces acute sun sensitivity.
Persistent UV injury in sun-exposed skin drives early freckle-like pigmentary change.
UV-sensitive ocular surface tissues contribute to photophobia in XP.
Defective repair in sun-exposed periocular and ocular-surface tissues causes the broader ocular disease burden.
Failed lesion bypass contributes to the distinctive mutational spectra of XP-V tumors.
edges.source: Defective error-free translesion synthesis in XP-V
edges.target: UV-signature mutagenesis and tumor mutation burden

edges.description: Extreme UV-driven mutagenesis promotes early basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma.
edges.source: UV-signature mutagenesis and tumor mutation burden

edges.description: In susceptible genotypes, DNA repair failure also contributes to neuronal damage and disability.
UV-signature mutagenesis promotes early basal cell carcinoma.
UV-signature mutagenesis promotes early cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma.
edges.target: Squamous cell carcinoma of the skin

edges.description: Progressive neurologic vulnerability includes sensorineural hearing loss in neurodegenerative XP subtypes.
Neuronal DNA-repair vulnerability contributes to axonal sensory and sensorimotor neuropathy.
Peripheral and central neurodegenerative involvement manifests with diminished reflexes.
Extrapyramidal involvement in neurologically affected XP genotypes can manifest as dystonia.
Progressive neuronal injury contributes to cognitive decline.

edges.source: Local treatment of premalignant and malignant skin lesions

edges.target: Defective error-free translesion synthesis in XP-V

edges.source: Biallelic loss-of-function or hypomorphic variants in NER genes

nodes.description: Basal cell carcinoma occurs at markedly earlier ages than in the general population as part of the sunlight-induced cancer phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: Xeroderma Pigmentosum.
evidence.snippet: Greatly increased risk of sunlight-induced cutaneous neoplasms (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, melanoma) within the first decade of life.

nodes.description: XP commonly results from pathogenic alleles that reduce nucleotide-excision repair capacity rather than from somatic events.
nodes.id: Biallelic loss-of-function or hypomorphic variants in NER genes

evidence.reference_title: Genomic mutation landscape of skin cancers from DNA repair-deficient xeroderma pigmentosum patients
evidence.snippet: Xeroderma pigmentosum (XP) is a genetic disorder caused by mutations in genes of the Nucleotide Excision Repair (NER) pathway (groups A-G).

nodes.description: Progressive cognitive impairment is part of the neurologic XP phenotype and is enriched in XPA, XPD, and XPG.

evidence.reference_title: Xeroderma Pigmentosum.
evidence.snippet: Approximately 25% of affected individuals have neurologic manifestations (acquired microcephaly, diminished or absent deep tendon stretch reflexes, progressive sensorineural hearing loss, progressive cognitive impairment, and ataxia).

evidence.reference_title: Neurological disease in xeroderma pigmentosum: prospective cohort study of its features and progression
evidence.snippet: XP results from biallelic mutations in any of eight genes involved in DNA repair systems, thus defining eight different genotypes (XPA, XPB, XPC, XPD, XPE, XPF, XPG and XP variant or XPV).

nodes.description: XP dramatically accelerates development of sunlight-induced basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanoma, often beginning in the first decade of life.

evidence.reference_title: Xeroderma Pigmentosum.
evidence.snippet: Greatly increased risk of sunlight-induced cutaneous neoplasms (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, melanoma) within the first decade of life.

nodes.description: Melanoma risk is markedly elevated because UV-induced lesions are not repaired normally.

evidence.reference_title: Xeroderma Pigmentosum.
evidence.snippet: Greatly increased risk of sunlight-induced cutaneous neoplasms (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, melanoma) within the first decade of life.

nodes.description: Deep intronic ERCC4 alleles reduce XPF expression through aberrant splicing or alternative polyadenylation and can present with early-onset skin cancers.

evidence.reference_title: Deep intronic founder mutations identified in the ERCC4/XPF gene are potential therapeutic targets for a high-frequency form of xeroderma pigmentosum
evidence.snippet: Both mutations cause a reduction of the ERCC4/XPF gene expression, resulting in XP clinical manifestations. Most cases developed early-onset skin cancers.

nodes.description: In XP-V, POLH deficiency impairs error-free translesion synthesis across UV photolesions, generating a distinct route to mutagenesis despite an intact excision machinery.
nodes.id: Defective error-free translesion synthesis in XP-V

evidence.reference_title: Genomic mutation landscape of skin cancers from DNA repair-deficient xeroderma pigmentosum patients
evidence.snippet: Mutational profile in XP-V tumors and experiments with POLH knockout cell line reveal the role of polymerase η in the error-free bypass of (i) rare TpG and TpA DNA lesions, (ii) 3' nucleotides in pyrimidine dimers, and (iii) TpT photodimers.

nodes.description: Dystonia is part of the extrapyramidal neurologic phenotype seen in the more severely neurologically affected XP subtypes.

evidence.reference_title: Neurological disease in xeroderma pigmentosum: prospective cohort study of its features and progression
evidence.snippet: Hyporeflexia, hypopallesthaesia, upper motor neuron signs, chorea, dystonia, oculomotor signs and cognitive impairment were frequent findings in XPA, XPD and XPG.

evidence.reference_title: Deep intronic founder mutations identified in the ERCC4/XPF gene are potential therapeutic targets for a high-frequency form of xeroderma pigmentosum
evidence.snippet: The first variant, located in intron 1, is a Japanese founder mutation, which additionally accounts for ~10% of the entire Japanese XP cases.

nodes.description: Freckle-like hyperpigmentation of the face develops in early childhood, especially in sun-exposed skin.

evidence.reference_title: Xeroderma Pigmentosum.
evidence.snippet: marked freckle-like pigmentation of the face before age two years

nodes.description: Reduced reflexes are frequent in the neurodegenerative XP subtypes, especially XPA, XPD, and XPG.

evidence.reference_title: Neurological disease in xeroderma pigmentosum: prospective cohort study of its features and progression
evidence.snippet: Hyporeflexia, hypopallesthaesia, upper motor neuron signs, chorea, dystonia, oculomotor signs and cognitive impairment were frequent findings in XPA, XPD and XPG.

nodes.description: Biallelic pathogenic variants in core XP genes compromise nucleotide-excision repair and prevent efficient removal of ultraviolet-induced bulky DNA lesions.

evidence.reference_title: Genomic mutation landscape of skin cancers from DNA repair-deficient xeroderma pigmentosum patients
evidence.snippet: Xeroderma pigmentosum (XP) is a genetic disorder caused by mutations in genes of the Nucleotide Excision Repair (NER) pathway (groups A-G) or in Translesion Synthesis DNA polymerase η (V).

nodes.description: Actinic keratoses and skin cancers are managed with local therapies such as cryotherapy, topical field therapy, curettage, Mohs surgery, or surgical excision depending on lesion burden and recurrence risk.
nodes.id: Local treatment of premalignant and malignant skin lesions

evidence.reference_title: Xeroderma Pigmentosum.
evidence.snippet: Premalignant skin lesions such as actinic keratoses can be treated by freezing with liquid nitrogen; larger areas can be treated with field treatments such as topical 5-fluorouracil or imiquimod preparations. Rarely, therapeutic dermatome shaving or dermabrasion has been used; skin neoplasms can be treated (as in persons without XP) with electrodesiccation and curettage or surgical excision; skin cancers that are recurrent or in locations at high risk for recurrence are best treated with Mohs micrographic surgery.

nodes.description: Ocular disease is highly prevalent and includes periocular skin disease, ocular surface pathology, and ocular neoplasia requiring regular ophthalmologic surveillance.

evidence.reference_title: Ophthalmic Manifestations of Xeroderma Pigmentosum: A Perspective from the United Kingdom.
evidence.snippet: Ninety-three percent of XP patients in our cohort had ocular involvement, with 65% describing photophobia. The most common abnormalities were in the periocular skin and ocular surface, including interpalpebral conjunctival melanosis (44%) and conjunctival injection (43%). Eleven percent of patients had required treatment for periocular cancers and 2% for ocular surface cancers.

nodes.description: High-dose oral isotretinoin can reduce new skin-cancer formation in selected high-risk XP patients, but toxicity limits long-term use.

evidence.reference_title: Prevention of skin cancer in xeroderma pigmentosum with the use of oral isotretinoin.
evidence.snippet: During two years of treatment with isotretinoin, there were 25 tumors (mean, 5; range, 3 to 9), with an average reduction in skin cancers of 63 percent (P = 0.019).

nodes.description: Homozygous POLH p.Thr191Pro is a functionally characterized pathogenic missense variant that abrogates polymerase activity and impairs UV lesion bypass in XP-V.

evidence.reference_title: Homozygous substitution of threonine 191 by proline in polymerase η causes Xeroderma pigmentosum variant.
evidence.snippet: providing molecular evidence that the substitution abrogates polymerase activity and results in UV sensitivity through deficient damage bypass.

evidence.reference_title: Genomic mutation landscape of skin cancers from DNA repair-deficient xeroderma pigmentosum patients
evidence.snippet: Xeroderma pigmentosum (XP) is a genetic disorder caused by mutations in genes of the Nucleotide Excision Repair (NER) pathway (groups A-G) or in Translesion Synthesis DNA polymerase η (V).

nodes.description: Axonal sensory and sensorimotor neuropathies are common ancillary-test findings in neurologically involved XP patients, particularly in the neurodegenerative subtypes.

evidence.reference_title: Neurological disease in xeroderma pigmentosum: prospective cohort study of its features and progression
evidence.snippet: Cerebellar and global brain atrophy, axonal sensory and sensorimotor neuropathies, and sensorineural hearing loss were common findings in patients.

nodes.description: Ocular UV injury commonly causes photophobia and related ocular surface disease.

evidence.reference_title: Ophthalmic Manifestations of Xeroderma Pigmentosum: A Perspective from the United Kingdom.
evidence.snippet: Ninety-three percent of XP patients in our cohort had ocular involvement, with 65% describing photophobia.

nodes.description: Acute sun sensitivity with severe sunburn and persistent erythema on minimal sun exposure is a core early clinical feature.

evidence.reference_title: Xeroderma Pigmentosum.
evidence.snippet: Acute sun sensitivity (severe sunburn with blistering, persistent erythema on minimal sun exposure).

nodes.description: Progressive ataxia is a measurable component of XP neurologic disease and worsens over time in XPA and XPD.

evidence.reference_title: Neurological disease in xeroderma pigmentosum: prospective cohort study of its features and progression
evidence.snippet: SARA total scores significantly increased over time in XPD (0.91 points/year, 95% confidence interval: 0.61, 1.21) and XPA (0.63 points/year, 95% confidence interval: 0.38, 0.89).

nodes.description: In neurologically affected XP genotypes, neural cells show impaired DNA repair and heightened apoptosis, providing a mechanistic route to ataxia, hearing loss, and cognitive decline.

evidence.reference_title: Modeling xeroderma pigmentosum associated neurological pathologies with patients-derived iPSCs.
evidence.snippet: Mutation of XPA in either neural stem cells (NSCs) or neurons resulted in severe DNA damage repair defects, and these neural cells with mutant XPA were hyper-sensitive to DNA damage-induced apoptosis.

nodes.description: Lifelong photoprotection with strict sun avoidance, protective clothing, sunscreen, and UV reduction is the core management strategy in XP.

evidence.reference_title: Xeroderma pigmentosum: an updated review.
evidence.snippet: Strict and consistent sun avoidance and protection and early detection and treatment of premalignant and malignant skin lesions are the mainstays of management.

nodes.description: Progressive sensorineural hearing loss is a common neurologic manifestation, especially in XPA, XPD, and XPG disease.

evidence.reference_title: Xeroderma Pigmentosum.
evidence.snippet: Approximately 25% of affected individuals have neurologic manifestations (acquired microcephaly, diminished or absent deep tendon stretch reflexes, progressive sensorineural hearing loss, progressive cognitive impairment, and ataxia).

nodes.description: Cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma is a common malignant consequence of the XP mutagenic state.
nodes.id: Squamous cell carcinoma of the skin

evidence.reference_title: Xeroderma Pigmentosum.
evidence.snippet: Greatly increased risk of sunlight-induced cutaneous neoplasms (basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, melanoma) within the first decade of life.

nodes.description: Ultraviolet exposure creates DNA damage that XP cells cannot adequately process, making sunlight the dominant upstream trigger for disease biology in skin and ocular tissues.

evidence.reference_title: Xeroderma pigmentosum: an updated review.
evidence.snippet: Xeroderma pigmentosum is a condition of abnormal DNA repair of ultraviolet radiation-induced and oxidative DNA damage, which leads to increased skin cancer susceptibility.

nodes.description: Persistent unrepaired or inaccurately bypassed UV lesions produce the high mutational burden and UV-signature mutations that underlie XP skin-cancer susceptibility.
nodes.id: UV-signature mutagenesis and tumor mutation burden

evidence.reference_title: Genomic mutation landscape of skin cancers from DNA repair-deficient xeroderma pigmentosum patients
evidence.snippet: XP is associated with an increased skin cancer risk, reaching, for some groups, several thousand-fold compared to the general population.
